coredata.dc:title: Multi-deformable interpenetrating network thermosensitive hydrogel fluorescent device for real-time and visual detection of nitrite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lingfei
Lin, Dan
Xu, Shihao
Yang, Liang
Jiang, Changlong
coredata.dc:description: Functionalized thermosensitive hydrogel materials exhibit excellent properties for the fabrication of sensing devices that enable real-time visual detection of food safety duo to their good plasticity and powerful loading capacity. Here, a ratiometric fluorescent device based on an interpenetrating network (IPN) thermosensitive hydrogel was designed to embed functionalized Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) and Blue Carbon dots (BCDs) composites in a multi-network structure to build a sensitive hazardous material nitrite (NO2 -) chemsensor. The hydrogel was utilized poloxamer 407 (P407), lignin and cellulose to form stable IPN structure, which resulted in complementation and synergy, thereby strengthening its porous network structure. The combination of fluorescent nanoprobes with the porous network structure has the potential to enhance stable fluorescence signals and improve sensing sensitivity. Moreover, the thermosensitive liquid-solid transition characteristics of the hydrogel facilitate its preparation into diverse sensing devices following curing at room temperature. The hydrogel device, when combined with a smartphone system, converted image information into data information, thereby enabling the accurate quantification of NO2 - with a detection limit of 9.38 nM in 2 s. The designed multi-functional hydrogel device is capable of real-time differentiation of NO2 - dosage with the naked eye, offering a high-contrast, rapid-response sensing methodology for visual assessment of food freshness. This research contributes to the expansion of hydrogel materials applications and the detection of hazardous materials in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric sensing
Visual detection
Hydrogel materials
Interpenetrating network
Food safety
Nitrite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BCDs and Au NCs
Optimization of microstructure characterization and mechanism of NO2 - probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) BCDs, (B) Au NCs and (C) Au NCs+NO2 -. (D) Fluorescence intensity changes of BCDs and Au NCs after adding NO2 -. The UV-Vis spectra of (E) Au NCs, BCDs, NO2 -, Au NCs+NO2 -. The FT-IR spectra of (F) Au NCs、Au NCs+NO2 -.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe solution upon the exposure to different concentrations of NO2 -, (B) detection limit of proportional probe, (C) after adding different concentrations of NO2 -, the color of the probe solution changes, (D) the selectivity of sensor, (E) the selectivity and interference test of sensor, and (F) fluorescence photo of selectivity.

SEM data of four different hydrogel (A) Poloxamer 407 (B) Lignin (C) Cellulose (D) LCG-P407. Four different hydrogel (E) a: Poloxamer 407; b: Lignin, c: Cellulose, d: LCG-P407. And transparency of hydrogel. (F) Test of water loss rate of hydrogel. a: P407; b: Lignin, c: Cellulose, d: LCG-P407. (G) Swelling kinetics test of hydrogel (a: P407; b: Lignin, c: Cellulose, d: LCG-P407). (H) Thermogravimetric data characterization of: Poloxamer 407; b: Lignin, c: Cellulose, d: LCG-P407. (I) The zeta data of P407, Lignin, Cellulose, LCG-P407. (J) Laser confocal microscopy photograph: LCG-P407 hydrogel and Au NCs/LCG-P407 @ hydrogel effect on bacteria. (K) LCG-P407 hydrogel and Au NCs/LCG-P407 @ hydrogel schematic diagram of E. coli antibacterial test.

(A) The hydrogel and fluorescent color in liquid state at 4 °C, hydrogel and fluorescent color in gel state at 25 °C and hydrogel and fluorescent color in gel state after adding excessive NO2 - at 25 °C, (B) preparation of hydrogel gloves and (E) the display of gloves, (C) hydrogel gloves contact NO2 - pork that does not exceed the standard and (F) the display of gloves, (D) hydrogel gloves contact NO2 - excess pork and (G) the display of gloves, (H) as the concentration of NO2 - increases, the fluorescence color of the hydrogel changes. (I) Mobile phone sensor platform interface. (J) The linear relationship between the B/R value and the concentration of NO2 - (0–1.2 μM).

(A) Au NCs/LCG-P407 hydrogel sensor detects NO2 - photos with time and temperature changes. (B) Au NCs/LCG-P407 hydrogel sensor detects NO2 - graph with time and temperature changes. (C) Classification of safe and unsafe levels of NO2 -.
Mechanism of NO2 - ratio detection by dual emission probes.
Design of hydrogel interpenetrating network and color change of hydrogel binding probe.

head.ce:title: Multi-deformable interpenetrating network thermosensitive hydrogel fluorescent device for real-time and visual detection of nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Hefei, Anhui, 230031 P R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functionalized thermosensitive hydrogel materials exhibit excellent properties for the fabrication of sensing devices that enable real-time visual detection of food safety duo to their good plasticity and powerful loading capacity. Here, a ratiometric fluorescent device based on an interpenetrating network (IPN) thermosensitive hydrogel was designed to embed functionalized Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) and Blue Carbon dots (BCDs) composites in a multi-network structure to build a sensitive hazardous material nitrite (NO2 -) chemsensor. The hydrogel was utilized poloxamer 407 (P407), lignin and cellulose to form stable IPN structure, which resulted in complementation and synergy, thereby strengthening its porous network structure. The combination of fluorescent nanoprobes with the porous network structure has the potential to enhance stable fluorescence signals and improve sensing sensitivity. Moreover, the thermosensitive liquid-solid transition characteristics of the hydrogel facilitate its preparation into diverse sensing devices following curing at room temperature. The hydrogel device, when combined with a smartphone system, converted image information into data information, thereby enabling the accurate quantification of NO2 - with a detection limit of 9.38 nM in 2 s. The designed multi-functional hydrogel device is capable of real-time differentiation of NO2 - dosage with the naked eye, offering a high-contrast, rapid-response sensing methodology for visual assessment of food freshness. This research contributes to the expansion of hydrogel materials applications and the detection of hazardous materials in food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional hydrogel with porous polymer network structure and excellent customizability have attracted more and more attention [1,2]. Hydrogel can sense small changes in the external environment (e.g., temperature, pH, light, ionic strength, electric field, and stress) and respond to external stimuli by expanding and contracting their own volume. The type of stimulus to which the hydrogel responds determines its categorization. Thermosensitive, pH-sensitive, light-sensitive, and pressure-sensitive hydrogel are among the most common types [3]. Thermosensitive hydrogels exhibit an abrupt change in water absorption at the phase transition temperature. This temperature can be divided into two categories: low-temperature contraction and high-temperature contraction. Many studies have been conducted on these types of hydrogels in recent years. P407 has the property of thermosensitive in situ gel, high concentration (20 %−30 %) P407 aqueous solution has a special inverse thermal gel property, that is, the solution exists in the form of a sol at low temperature, and when the temperature rises (>25 ℃) when it becomes a gel state [4]. The product can be used in conjunction with a variety of liquid devices and can be attached to the surface of the device following the process of high-temperature gelation, thus enabling adaptation to a range of potential applications in diverse environmental contexts [5]. At present, the application research of thermosensitive hydrogel mainly focuses on heat-shrinkable thermosensitive hydrogel, and through copolymerization or interpenetrating network and other means to improve the strength and responsiveness of the gel, nevertheless the mechanical strength of hydrogel itself is poor, especially in the water dissolved basically lost its own support ability, and its response speed is slow, thus largely limiting its application [6].

Food safety have become important issues in lives that cannot be ignored. Simple, fast, low-cost and accurate food safety detection systems are becoming more and more indispensable to cope with the proliferation of food safety issues. The World Health Organization (WHO) has set certain dose requirements for different types of hazards [7]. In recent years, inspectors have been able to identify the presence of hazards in the environment by using traditional techniques such as electrochemistry [8], colorimetry [9], UV–visible absorption [10], chemiluminescence [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], spectrophotometry [13,14] and chromatography [15]. These reported methods often suffer from cumbersome detection procedures, expensive instrumentation, poor visual semi-quantitative capability or time-consuming problems, which collectively impede their practical application. In contrast to these methods, fluorescence sensing methods are strong candidates for analysis due to their simplicity, rapidity, high sensitivity and ease of visualization [16]. Among them, NO2 - is used as a typical additive in food products, and human intake of NO2 - leads to the irreversible conversion of hemoglobin in the blood to methemoglobin, which impairs the oxygen transport system [17–21]. In addition, once NO2 - enters the stomach through food, it also reacts with secondary amines and amides in the body to form carcinogenic N-nitrosamines. To protect public health, the WHO has set the maximum dose of NO2 - in drinking water as 1.0 mg/L, and the residual amount of NO2 - in meat products cannot exceed 30 mg/kg [22]. Zhou et al. prepared fluorescent copper nanoclusters using glutathione as a stabilizer and ascorbic acid as a reducing agent. Further studies showed that NO2 - quenched the fluorescence of the concentration range of 10–225 μM, with a detection limit of 3.4 μM [23]. Yang et al. constructed a ternary ZnCdS QDs based on fluorescence sensor for NO2 - detection. With the introduction of NO2 -, the fluorescence emission intensity of ZnCdS QDs gradually decreases, and the LOD is as low as 505 nM [24]. Zhang et al. developed a quenched fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of NO2 - in water-based media. The probe passes a distinct visual color change from colorless to pink with a LOD of 0.1 μM [25]. Fluorescent probe detection alone lacks application, therefore hydrogel encapsulated fluorescent probes were designed to detect aliased food safety.

To circumvent the potential for secondary pollution resulting from the detection process, it is imperative that the selected materials for the hydrogel are themselves non-toxic. At present, Au NCs, a novel type of nanomaterial, have garnered considerable interest in the construction of fluorescent nanosensors due to their favorable characteristics, including good water solubility, low toxicity, and an ultra-small size [26]. While synthesizing and utilizing metal nanoclusters (NCs), using ligands to protect metal NCs from aggregation is a key step to ensure sensing performance. Since NCs covered with protein showed stable optical properties and excellent biocompatibility, protein was chosen as the ideal ligand [27–29]. Beyond that, BCDs are a powerful substitute for semiconductor quantum dots, whose properties can be adjusted by controlling size and composition. Especially, the various synthesis methods make their practical utilization more attractive in environmental and health surveillance [30–35]. Nevertheless, to knowledge, fluorescent sensors based on biomass-derived BCDs and Au NCs have not been reported for detecting NO2 -. Considering the simplicity, economy and non-toxic, it is promising to design a novel fluorescent nanoprobe for NO2 - monitor based on biomass-derived BCDs and Au NCs.

Here, a selective and sensitive thermal hydrogel fluorescent sensor (LCG-P407) based on blue-emitting BCDs synthesized using lemon and red-emitting Au NCs is proposed for visual removal of NO2 -. The initial observation of the prepared sensor revealed the presence of red fluorescence at 647 nm and blue fluorescence at 461 nm, both under 365 nm excitation. In the presence of NO2 -, the red-emitting Au NCs, which serve as the detection signal, undergo a burst release, while the blue-emitting BCDs, which function as the reference signal, remain insensitive to NO2 -. The striking red to blue fluorescence color evolution enabled instant detection of NO2 - by the naked eye under UV light without any sophisticated instrumentation. The limit of detection (LOD) was 9.38 nM, which is well below the maximum level (21.74 μM) of NO2 - in drinking water (WHO). Moreover, a multitude of portable optical devices were further developed in conjunction with a smartphone integration platform, considering the qualitative and quantitative requirements pertinent to the field. By combining the thermosensitive hydrogel with IPN formed by P407, lignin and cellulose resulted in the creation of a visual fluorescence detection hydrogel, which enabled the on-site visual quantitative detection of NO2 - in real samples. This development offers a novel approach to the detection of small molecule contaminants in the context of food safety control and hazardous material monitoring.

Gold tetrachloride (HAuCl4·4 H2O, 99.9 %), Poloxamer 407 (99 %), lignin (99 %), cellulose (99 %), Luria-Bertani agar (LB agar, 99 %), PBS buffer solution (pH=3.6, 99.99 %), NH3·H2O (99.2 %), NaCl (99 %), ZnCl2 (99 %), FeCl2 (98 %), CuCl2 (99 %), CdCl2 (98 %), HgCl2 (99 %), AlCl3 (99 %), FeCl3 (99 %), Na2SO4 (95 %), H3PO4 (96 %), NaHSO4 (99 %), NaHCO3 (99 %), NaNO3 (99 %), HCl (99.7 %), Na2S (99 %), AsCl3 (99 %),were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922) (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923) (S. aureus) were purchased from HuiBo medical company, eggs and lemons were obtained from the local market (China). Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was produced with a Millipore water purification system.

UV−vis absorption spectrophotometer was recorded by Cary UV–vis Compact. Fluorescence spectra were carried out on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. The Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) samples were observed using a JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscope. Samples were observed using an Apreo SEM scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 high-performance electron spectrometer. The data of antimicrobial properties were obtained by laser confocal microscopy. The 3D model was designed by SolidWorks 2016 (Dassault Co., Ltd.) and printed by the S140 micro and nano 3D printing system (Shenzhen Magic Square New Material Technology Co., Ltd.). Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA, Perkin-Elmer Corporation, USA). The photos were taken under an AGL-9406 portable UV lamp with a Canon EOS 600D digital camera. 3D printer (Makerbot Replicator 2X). The smartphone integration platform is composed of RGB software (RGB Test, Zbigniew Osada) and iPhone X (Apple Inc.), which takes fluorescent photos by the phone for RGB reading and converts them into concentration information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BCDs and Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: 20 mL of pulp-free lemon juice was mixed with 10 mL of water to form a transparent solution. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL stainless-steel autoclave lined with teflon and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the BCDs solution was dialyzed (cut-off Mn: 500–1000 Da) against deionized water for 24 h to remove small molecules [36]. The blue carbon dot power was obtained after freeze-dry of the carbon quantum dots solution.

All glassware used in the synthesis of Au NCs is cleaned with aqua regia (HCl/HNO3 = 3:1, v/v) and thoroughly rinsed with ultra-pure water and ethanol prior to use. Simply put, the egg whites are first isolated from the egg and then diluted 30 times with deionized water. The diluted mixture was stirred at room temperature for 15 min, then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting clear solution (10 mL) was stirred with an aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (10 mL, 5 mmol L−1) at room temperature for 5 min, and then an aqueous solution of NaOH was added to the mixture under constant agitation until slightly alkaline and stirred at room temperature for another 5 min. The mixture is then transferred to a domestic microwave oven (power: 90 W) and heated for about 5 min until the fluorescence intensity of the sample reaches its maximum. The resulting Au NCs was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm/min for 10 min to remove large particles, then centrifuged at 4500 rpm/min using a filter with a retention mass of 30000 Da for 20 min and rinsed with deionized water (4 mL ×5). The residual Au NCs on the filter membrane were freeze-dried, the dried powder was collected, prepared into a solution, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use [37].

Add 40 μL Au NCs, 20 μL BCDs, 30 μL PBS buffer solution (pH=3.6), and different concentrations of NO2 - solution (10 μL) were added in sequence to 1900 μL water. After being reacted for 3 s, the reaction solution was removed to a quartz cuvette. And the fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe under a single wavelength excitation at 365 nm were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. All the sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate. The color changes were observed under a 365 nm UV lamp.

To detect the selectivity of the probe, a series of ions (NH4 +, Na+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, HSO4 -, H2PO4 -, SO4 2-, HCO3 -, NO3 -, Cl-), instead of NO2 -, were selected to be mixed with the ratio probe. The concentration of interference ions was 1.0 μM, respectively. For the interference study, the 1.0 μM NO2 - was further added to the probe solution, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded again.

Firstly, 80 μL Au NCs, 40 μL BCDs, 60 μL PBS buffer solution (pH=3.6), and different concentrations of NO2 - solution (20 μL) were added in sequence to 3800 μL water. Then 1 g P407 was dissolved in the above solution and stirred to dissolve, and 0.1 g lignin and 0.1 g cellulose were added and mix-stirred. The stirring time is about 2 h and the temperature is room temperature. Finally, the stirred solution will be gradually cross-linked to form an IPN thermosensitive hydrogel, and the resulting hydrogel solution will be stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

E. coli and S. aureus were selected as representatives of Gram-negative and positive bacteria for the bacteriostatic assay. One bacterial colony was selected from the LB agar plate, and the bacteria were placed in the LB culture solution and placed in a shaker at 150 rpm, 37 °C, and incubated for 8 h. 0.1 mL of the hydrogel material was aspirated, 0.9 mL of the bacterial solution was added to a round-bottomed test tube, and the mixture was mixed homogeneously, and co-cultivated for 2 h in a shaker at 150 rpm, 37 °C. For control, 0.1 mL of the PBS solution. After the incubation was completed, 10 μL of the mixed solution was evenly applied to the solid medium and incubated in an incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. Each group was experimented three times. The antimicrobial performance of the hydrogel was evaluated by counting the number of colonies on the solid medium.

To evaluate the feasibility and repeatability of the proposed sensor in practical applications, we further evaluated the effectiveness of the ratio fluorescence sensor in detecting NO2 - in real samples. Among them, lake water sample: filtered with a 220 nm microfiltration film to remove insoluble impurities, and the filtered lake water can be tested and used. Bacon: chopped and washed with saline to remove blood stains, and then washed with distilled water. Weigh the minced bacon samples into a clean centrifuge tube, add 5 % formic acid acetonitrile solution, and then add a certain amount of NaCl, centrifuge at 2500 r/min for 10 min, 8000 r/min for 2 min, and take the supernatant. Then, the supernatant was filtered with a 220 nm microfiltration film to remove insoluble impurities, and the filtered body fluid was obtained for detection. Pickle: a certain amount of pickle, washed with distilled water, and dried. Take a part of chopped pickle, centrifuge at 5000 r/min for 5 min, filter and save the supernatant. The supernatant was then filtered with a 220 nm microfiltration film to remove insoluble impurities, and the filtered supernatant was ready for detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of microstructure characterization and mechanism of NO2 - probe
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism for NO2 - detection were present here. As shown in Scheme 1, the as-prepared Au NCs exhibit bright red emission without aggregation under the protection of protein. In the PBS buffer (pH=3.6), upon introducing NO2 -, the diazonium reaction between Au NCs and NO2 - leads to the formation of diazonium salts. The diazotization reaction is firstly composed of -NH2 on the surface of Au NCs combined with NO2 -, the electrophile attacks nitrogen atoms to generate unstable intermediates, and then through a series of proton transfers, the unstable intermediates are rapidly decomposed and protons are released, and finally generate diazonium salts, the specific reaction process can be seen in Video S1 [38,39]. The diazonium salt is an electron-withdrawing group, the n-electron lone pair electron cloud is not coplanar with the π-electron cloud on the group, and the n→π * transition is forbidden, resulting in the aggregated fluorescence quenching of Au NCs [40]. Additionally, the blue-emitting fluorescence from CDs act as a good reference background with almost no change when exposed to NO2 -. The two-emission intensity vary and display color changes from red to blue, thus generating ratiometric fluorescence signals for quantitative and visual detection of the analyte.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.164017.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.135471.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2
.

The morphologies of the BCDs, Au NCs and Au NCs + NO2 - were studied by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, the as-prepared BCDs exhibit high monodispersed and relatively uniform distribution with mean diameter of 2.0 nm (Fig. S1). In Fig. 1B The Au NCs was monodispersed with the diameter 7–9 nm, which is slightly smaller than the DLS data (Fig. S2A), proving the better hydrophilic properties. After introducing NO2 -, the diazonium salt in the form of electron withdrawing groups is produced due to the diazotization reaction, which leads to the agglomeration of Au NCs. In Fig. 1C that the Au NCs are agglomerated with the intrinsic size not changing significantly (Fig. S2B), which is consistent with the sensing mechanism demonstrating the reaction between Au NCs and NO2 -. Moreover, optical properties of the sensing system are investigated. The excitation wavelength can be obtained from Fig. S3, 350 nm as the optimal excitation. In Fig. 1D that after the addition of NO2 -, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs remains unchanged, while that of Au NCs decreases with the gradual increase of NO2 -, showing a fluorescence color change from red to blue, thus constructing a ratio fluorescence probe. The UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 1E displays the absorption band arising from the absorption of proteins immobilized on the surface of Au NCs appears at a wavelength of ∼280 nm. Fig. 1F shows the FT-IR spectrum of the Au NCs and Au NCs + NO2 -. The significant peak appeared at around 2280 cm−1 after adding NO2 - arose from the diazo bond [41], which proved that diazotization reaction successfully occurred (FT-IR of BCDs are shown in Fig. S4), and the infrared characteristic peak at 1031 cm−1 may be -NH2/-NH. XPS tests were also performed on Au NCs, Au NCs + NO2 -, BCDs, which confirmed the presence of Au, C, N, O elements. The XPS splitting of elemental N can be clearly seen in the originally -NH2 and C-N peaks, after undergoing the reaction with NO2 -, the peaks are obtained as N = N/CN and NN. The spectrum of Au in the 4 f region can be divided into two obvious peaks, namely Au 4 f7/2 and Au 4 f5/2, each of which corresponds to 84.13 and 87.83 eV and 84.78 and 88.27 eV. It can be seen from Fig. S5 that comparing with the FT-IR data, it can clearly prove the formation of diazo bonds.

Prior to apply the proposed method to detection, several experimental parameters such as pH, temperature, probe of concentration ratio are further optimized. Firstly, the effect of different pH values on the fluorescence and detection effect of nanoprobes after adding NO2 - was studied. It can be observed from Fig. S6 that when pH< 5, the intensity ratio of I461/I647 for Au NCs-BCDs remains constant. When pH> 5, because the system needs to be in acidic conditions to form diazo compounds, the experimental unstable. To obtain optimal determination, the pH is finally fixed at 3.6 in buffer. And it can be proved that the probe remains relatively stable within 300 min (Fig. S7). The fluorescence intensity ratio was observed over a temperature range of 0–50 °C. As shown in the Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity ratios had no obvious change, implying the excellent stability and ant-photobleaching. For the further practical application, a physiology temperature of 37 °C is selected as the optimal temperature. To optimize the conditions for visualization, the response of different fluorescence intensity ratios (I461/I647) to NO2 - was meticulously investigated. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs does not change after the addition of NO2 -, whereas the fluorescence intensity of Au NCs gradually decreases with the addition of NO2 -. For the intensity ratios such as 1/1.5, 1/2, 1/3 and 1/3.5, the result demonstrated that the intensity ratio value of 1/3 is the best for visual detection (Fig. S10).

Fig. 2A illustrates the fluorescence spectra of NO2 - detection employing the ratiometric fluorescent Au NCs-BCDs sensor. As the concentration of NO2 - increased (0–1.2 μM), the blue emission at 461 nm remained constant while the red emission at 647 nm gradually decreased, resulting in a successive color change from red to pink to blue. These visible color changes can be observed in Video S3. The corresponding fluorescence photos in Fig. 2C confirm the feasibility of visual detection of NO2 -. In Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity ratio I461/I647 of Au NCs-BCDs sensor was proportional to NO2 - concentration in the range of 0–1.2 μM, with y = 1.5679x + 0.0990, R2 = 0.9914. According to the formula 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation and k is the slope of the regression equation, the LOD for NO2 - is as low as 9.38 nM [42]. Excellent detection limit and response time compared to other articles (Table S1). The increase in the fluorescence ratio of I461/I647 is attributed to the disappearance of red fluorescence, which is consistent with the sensing mechanism of Au NCs-BCDs. In actual detection process, various environmental interferent substances could affect the detection performance, and even lead to false response signals. Selected several cations (Cd2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, NH4 +, Na+, K+, AS3+) and anions (Cl-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, HCO3 -, HClO-, S2-), which may react with the analyte and each other, resulting in changes in fluorescence intensity. As portrayed in Fig. 2D, no significant changes in fluorescence spectra were observed upon the adding these interfering species. And the coexistence of these resulted in negligible interference. Fig. 2E further discusses the selectivity and interference performance. All the interfering substances had no fluorescence response, nevertheless the addition of NO2 - triggered significant fluorescence intensity ratio I461/I647 enhancement. As illustrated in Fig. 2F that compared with other interfering ions, NO2 - can induce significant fluorescent color change of the sensing system, which can be easily recognized under UV lamp. Therefore, the fluorescent sensor provided a promising method to qualitatively identify NO2 - with high selectivity, and has potential application prospects in various fields.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.135471.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S3.
Video S3
.

P407 has the property of thermosensitive in situ gelation, which is liquid at low temperatures and solid at high temperatures, and different concentrations of P407 will also influence the gelation effect, therefore chose 20 % concentration of P407 hydrogel to be used as a substrate (Fig. S11). The hydrogel can be used in combination with a variety of liquid devices, and it can be attached to the surface of the device after high-temperature gelation to adapt to a variety of environmental applications. However, the mechanical strength of the hydrogel itself is poor, especially after being swollen by water, it basically loses its own supporting ability, and the reaction speed is slow, which largely limits its application, and the strength and response towards nitrate ions of the gel can only be improved by copolymerization or interpenetrating network. As shown in Scheme 2, IPN hydrogel is a unique interpenetrating network polymer formed by two or more polymers through physical or chemical cross-linking and entanglement IPN hydrogel by means of network interpenetration, two functionally different polymers can form a stable combination, realizing the complementary and synergistic effect of component properties. Both lignin and cellulose have high molecular weight and stability, and have some special physical and chemical properties. Among them, cellulose has many hydroxyl groups in its molecular structure, which can be connected into a network through the form of hydrogen bonding to form cellulose-based hydrogel by physical cross-linking. The structure of lignin contains functional groups such as aliphatic hydroxyl, phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, and the C3 and C5 positions in its aromatic region are also the active sites for chemical reactions. Lignin can help the composite hydrogel to form a porous structure to accommodate more water molecules. IPN hydrogel were designed to improve the strength and response towards nitrate ions of thermosensitive hydrogel. Based on satisfying the P407 hydrogel, the excellent properties of lignin and cellulose were introduced into the system to make the designed hydrogel more widely used.

Fig. 3A-C shows the SEM data of three kinds of doped freeze-dried hydrogel respectively. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 3A that the surface of P407 freeze-dried hydrogel alone has no obvious holes, and the surface is continuous nevertheless uneven, indicating that it has large pores and cannot be well combined with the probe for detection. Fig. 3B is lignin hydrogel in a rod-like material with a hole in the middle, and its dispersion is chaotic. As shown in Fig. 3C, are cellulose hydrogel with obvious fibrous structure. However, lignin and cellulose alone cannot meet the needs of hydrogel. As can be seen from Fig. 3D, the honeycomb morphology is obvious, which can be well combined with the probe, and has advantages such as high temperature sensitivity and flexibility that other hydrogel alone does not have, DLS data can also further validate the properties of hydrogel (Fig. S12). DLS can be used to evaluate the stability of polymer solutions to help optimize material preparation processes and application properties. It can be seen from Fig. S12 that the size of LCG-P407 does not increase even after the Cellulose with large DLS size is added. The smaller DLS size proves the stability of IPN connections and the excellent structural properties of LCG-P407.

Fig. 3E shows the transmittance data of three undoped hydrogel and the blended LCG-P407 hydrogel. It can be clearly seen from the figure that the transmittance of the single lignin hydrogel is very poor. Considering that the transmittance of hydrogel will have a certain impact on the fluorescence detection of the probe, hydrogel with high transmittance is required. Therefore, the synthesized LCG-P407 hydrogel can meet application requirements. Fig. 3F shows the test of hydrogel's water loss rate. In the long run, the development of anti-water loss hydrogel is only a preliminary exploration to develop complex and changeable environmental adaptive hydrogel. Most hydrogel materials are susceptible to water loss and desiccation under standard conditions, resulting in the loss of numerous functional attributes, including elasticity, conductivity, transparency, and self-adaptability. For thermosensitive hydrogel, freezing/loss of water in the hydrogel can cause it to lose elasticity or weaken its sensitivity, severely limiting its application. As illustrated in the figure, the LCG-P407 hydrogel displays an excellent capacity to resist water loss. Fig. 3G illustrates the swelling rate test of the hydrogel. A thermosensitive hydrogel must possess both a cross-linked network structure and a certain degree of hydrophilicity. The structure contains a substantial quantity of water, which enables swelling to occur in an environment with water as a dispersive medium. The gel contains a proportion of hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups, which are susceptible to alteration in response to changes in temperature. These alterations can affect the hydrophobic action of the groups and the strength of the hydrogen bonds between the macromolecular chains, leading to structural changes and volume fluctuations in the gel. It was observed that an increase in the cross-linking density under identical conditions resulted in a reduction in the degree of swelling. Accordingly, to produce a hydrogel with a robust cross-linking density, it is essential to regulate its degree of swelling. As illustrated in the figure, the LCG-P407 hydrogel exhibits an excellent anti-swelling rate. Fig. 3H illustrates that the hydrogel exhibits robust thermal stability at 100 °C, even in a dehydrated state, withstanding temperatures up to 250 °C. This durability is sufficient for typical daily use. A higher absolute value of the zeta potential indicates a greater electrostatic repulsion between particles, which in turn signifies enhanced physical stability and a more stable hydrogel. As illustrated in Fig. 3I, the absolute value of the zeta potential of the LCG-P407 hydrogel is notably high, substantiating its exceptional physical stability.

Considering that the actual use process needs to contact different food surfaces, it is necessary to test the antibacterial performance of hydrogel. Bacterial death was further characterized by laser confocal microscopy. After taking 0.9 mL of bacterial solution and mixing it with 0.1 mL of hydrogel for incubation respectively, 1 μL of DAPI and PI were added and incubated for 30 min the fluorescence channels of DAPI and PI were excited by 405 nm and 543 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3J, through the live-dead experiment of the bacterial solution can be obtained in the control group can obviously see no death of bacteria, and then add LCG-P407 hydrogel after some bacteria died, showing the bacterial inhibition performance of LCG-P407 hydrogel, when then add Au NCs, the bacterial inhibition effect is better, and most of the bacteria are dead. As can be seen from the Fig. 3K also demonstrated that the survival rate of E. coli gradually decreased (from 100 % to 7.5 %) with the addition of LCG-P407 hydrogel and Au NCs. At the same time, we also conducted antibacterial experiments on S. aureus. It can be clearly seen from Fig. S13 that the survival rate of S. aureus gradually decreased with the addition of LCG-P407 hydrogel and Au NCs (from 100 % to 4.1 %). The results showed that Au NCs/LCG-P407 @ hydrogel had excellent antibacterial properties.

Demonstration of hydrogel gloves in the detection of NO2 -. Benefiting from the attractive unique properties of functional modification and sol-gel transition, the hydrogel-based glove sensor has been demonstrated in the detection of NO2 -. The 25 % concentration of P407 can undergo a sol-gel transition by increasing the temperature above 4 °C, accompanied by a long-term gelling mechanism. More importantly, P407 possess good solubilizing ability, low toxicity, and compatibility [43]. Consequently, when on the surface of skin, it can remain gel state without excessive temperature handling and is not harmful to human body. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, the non-toxic nanoprobe-modified hydrogel remained liquid at 4 °C with a thickness of about 0.11 cm, and turned into a gel state when the temperature was increased to 25 °C, and the thickness remained unchanged. Simultaneously, both liquid and gelatinous hydrogel modified with non-toxic nanoprobe exhibited red-emitting fluorescence under UV irradiation. Upon exposure to NO2 -, the hydrogel turned to blue obviously. It can be seen from Fig. S14 that the hydrogel exhibits an obvious liquid gel state when the temperature is lower than 25 °C, and changes to gel state when the temperature rises to 25 °C, with a very fast speed, which also supports the change from solution state to gel state in Fig. 4 A.

Displaying a set of excellent performances enabled by P407 hydrogel modified with non-toxic nanoprobe, prepared hydrogel gloves also maintain the endowing superior stability and essential capacity for NO2 - detection. Since LCG-P407 Hydrogel is a heat-sensitive hydrogel and is not easily formed, LCG-P407 hydrogel is applied to the surface of the glove to form a thin layer that is responsible for contacting and detecting NO2 - (Fig. 4B). As depicted in Fig. 4E, P407 hydrogel modified with fluorescent nanoprobe showed an obvious and quick sol-gel transition under the human body temperature > 36 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 4 C, chose the pork as experimental sample. When contacting pork without NO2 -, it was obvious that the gloves appear red fluorescence (Fig. 4 F), proving that pork was safe for human consumption. When the hydrogel gloves contacted the pork with excessive NO2 - content (Fig. 4D, add KNO2), it can be seen from Fig. 4 G that the hydrogel glove responded rapidly in 2 s as the NO2 - content exceeds the standard (Fig. S15), and exhibited blue fluorescence, and the pork is judged to be high in NO2 -. And it was clearly seen from Fig. 4H that with the increasing amounts of NO2 -, the fluorescence color of the hydrogel gloves under UV light was gradually converted from red to blue. Therefore, P407 hydrogel-based gloves sensors here still maintain an efficient detection function without any complex operation, giving gloves sensor portability to the complex various samples and manifesting no toxicity, can get an updated understanding of the hydrogel sensor via Video S3. Meanwhile the portable smartphone integrated light-sensing platform consists of a removable smartphone connection device built with 3D printing technology and an APP programmed. The specific 3D printing design and build device are shown in Fig. S16. The smartphone-based NO2 - fluorescence detection platform is realized through three steps: image acquisition, signal detection and quantitative analysis.

Arbitrarily captured color photos can be quickly captured and accurately converted to the corresponding RGB values by APP, as shown in Fig. 4I, and the concentration is calculated. Then as shown in Fig. 4 J. The Blue-channel/Red-channel (B/R) values showed a good linear relationship with a linear coefficient of R2 = 0.9847 at NO2 - concentrations of 0–1.2 μM. Therefore, the B/R values can achieve quantitative detection of NO2 - with a detection limit of 9.84 nM. Compared with the direct detection of NO2- by using the probe, the detection limit obtained by using the sensor is close to the detection limit of the probe, and the detection time is also close, so the sensor has better detection ability. The above results show that the simple, portable, and real-time NO2 - detection method achieves an ultrasensitive detection. sensitive detection, which can provide technical support for public health and food safety protection.

To further improve its practicability and reproducibility, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was further evaluated for detection NO2 - in real samples. The fluorescence sensor based on the prepared nanoprobe can realize the visual detection of NO2 -, the evaluation was carried out with standard addition methods and recovery experiments. A range of concentrations of NO2 - (0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 μM) were added into real samples of lake water, bacon, and pickle as shown in Table S2. The detailed recovery values were calculated to be from 94.0 to 106.0 % based on the detection results, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 0.38 to 4.13 %, indicating good accuracy and reliability. The results show that the developed sensing system is suitable for practical applications in water and food samples. In addition, the Au NCs/LCG-P407 hydrogel can also be designed for the tracer of NO2 - content to meet the needs of food safety. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence color of hydrogel also gradually changes from red to blue. With the increase of time, the NO2 - content in bacon and pickle (4 °C and 25 °C) gradually increases. This results in concentrations exceeding international standards bacon (0.64 μM) and pickle (0.43 μM). As shown in Fig. 5B, it is obvious that the NO2 - content of bacon and pickle increases sharply when the temperature and time increase. As shown in Fig. 5 C, by classifying the safety and insecurity of NO2 - content, it is obvious that bacon and pickle are safe within 4 °C and 12 h, nevertheless bacon and pickle are not safe beyond 3 h when they are at 25 °C [44,45]. In this way, the hydrogel sensor is also used to trace the NO2 - content and improve the guarantee of food safety.

In conclusion, a functionalized thermosensitive hydrogel was successfully developed to sensitively acquire NO2 - information. During the gelation process, the functionalized Au NCs and BCDs composites are incorporated into the multi-network structure of the hydrogel, exhibiting distinctive fluorescence response behavior and operational stability. It is noteworthy that the crosslinked P407, lignin and cellulose imparted to the multi-network hydrogel an excellent porous network structure, which was a practical and important property. The porous network structure of the LCG-P407 fluorescent hydrogel enables the detection of NO2 - over a linear range of 0–1.2 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 9.38 nM and a detection time of less than 2 s. A homemade portable device, consisting of a 3D printed accessory and a smartphone, was used to convert the fluorescence images of the hydrogel into data information. This reflected the accurate quantification of NO2 - with a LOD of 9.84 nM, which was close to the detection limit of the actual measurement. The portable device was also used to assess the concentration of NO2 - in meat. In conclusion, a novel Au-NCs/LCG-P407 fluorescent hydrogel was developed in this study with the aim of optimizing portable and efficient device strategies and providing a basis for the further development of sensing platforms for environmental and hazardous substance detection in food safety.

Nitrite (NO2 -) is a common additive, but excessive intake of NO2 - can cause damage to the oxygen transport system. Consequently, it’s of paramount importance to develop a rapid and accurate method for the determination of NO2 - content. This paper presents a novel interpenetrating network thermo-hydrogel proportional fluorescence device, embedded with Au NCs and BCDs, which has been designed to construct a sensor with a detection limit of 9.38 nM within 2 s. The multi-functional hydrogel device can identify NO2 - content in real time, thereby offering a rapid response sensing method for the visual detection of food freshness and environmental safety.

Lingfei Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dan Lin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. changlong Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization. Shihao Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program (2021YFD2000202),National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos, 22274158), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2208085MB30) and Anhui Provincial Key Research and Development Program (2022k07020010).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found. Supporting Information Available: Include analysis of FT-IR, DLS, optimal excitation curve, optimal emission curve, fluorescence detection curve, and XPS data of BCDs and Au NCs. Characterization of pH value, temperature, optimal fluorescence ratio, and stability of fluorescence sensing system, literature comparison of fluorescent probes, hydrogel schematic diagram of S. aureus antibacterial test, phase transition temperature, DLS, concentration, reaction time, 3D printing design, and real sample detection of LCG-P407 hydrogel.
